behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix
behaviors.justification: A unit test of filename sorting logic doesn't catch the real failure: that the runner might sort filenames correctly but then execute them in wrong order due to async handling or other bugs. The failure boundary is at the database level — if migrations execute out of order, later migrations fail or schema becomes corrupted. A mock of the SQL execution layer could always report 'executed in order' even if the runner shuffled them. You need a real database to verify statements actually executed in the correct sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real PostgreSQL test database with three migrations named V1_create_users.sql, V3_add_status.sql, V2_add_email.sql. Execute the runner and verify the _migrations table records them in order V1, V2, V3 (not alphabetical). Additionally, verify that V2 can successfully reference structures created by V1, proving the order was correct at execution time, not just in the metadata table.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test must verify actual order of execution against a real database, not just that a sorting function works. A mock would hide bugs in how the runner applies the sorted list.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run
behaviors.justification: A mock of the 'check if applied' logic could pass even if the implementation is buggy (wrong WHERE clause, missing lock, stale check). The real failure is database state corruption — duplicate application of a non-idempotent migration could corrupt data or fail with constraint errors. You need a real database to verify: (a) the _migrations table is consulted correctly on the second run, (b) the migration is truly skipped, and (c) the operation (e.g., table creation) happened exactly once. This also catches subtle bugs where a partial rollback on the first run leaves _migrations unupdated.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the runner against a real PostgreSQL test database with one migration file. Verify it applied and appears in _migrations. Then run the runner a second time and verify: the migration does not appear twice in _migrations, the underlying operation (e.g., table exists and has the correct row count) shows only one execution, and no error was thrown on the second run due to idempotency violation.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A mock of the database could pretend to check and skip, but you need persistent state verification. Only a real database proves the second run did not re-apply.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted
behaviors.justification: A mock of transaction handling could pretend to rollback while the real database layer never actually wrapped the migration in a transaction. The real failure is database-level state corruption when a transaction boundary is missing or incorrect. You must execute actual SQL against a real database, deliberately cause a failure, and inspect the database state to confirm no partial state was left behind. Mocking the rollback behavior would hide the bug entirely.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real PostgreSQL test database with three migrations where V2 intentionally fails (e.g., duplicate key, constraint violation). Execute the runner and verify: (a) the changes from V2 are rolled back by querying the database (confirm the table or data V2 modified is not persisted), (b) V3 did not execute (confirm it does not appear in _migrations and its changes are absent), (c) only V1 appears in _migrations. Re-run to confirm V2 is retried and fails consistently.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Failure boundary is at the database transaction level. Cannot verify rollback with a mock — you must inspect actual database state after a failure.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances
behaviors.justification: A mock of database locking or an in-process async simulation would not catch real OS-level race conditions or actual PostgreSQL locking behavior. The real failure only manifests when two actual processes concurrently access the database with true timing non-determinism. You must spawn separate OS processes to create the timing window where both can read 'not applied' before either writes 'applied'. Mocking would serialize access and always pass. Additionally, database-level pessimistic or optimistic locking mechanisms can only be verified against a real database with real concurrent connections.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn two separate Node.js processes, each executing the runner against the same real PostgreSQL test database at approximately the same time. Introduce a deliberate delay in the 'check if applied' logic to increase the race condition window. After both processes complete, verify: (a) each migration appears exactly once in _migrations (no duplicates), (b) no database constraint violations or partial state remains, (c) both processes exited successfully. Repeat this test 10+ times to account for timing variations and increase the likelihood of catching the race.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: True OS concurrency and real database locking are required. A mock would serialize by default, hiding the race. Integration-level testing with async/await might partially work, but true process-level timing is necessary for realistic failure detection.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied
behaviors.justification: A mock of 'create table' could reorder execution and you'd never know. The real failure is if the runner tries to INSERT into _migrations before CREATE TABLE runs, causing a crash or skipped insert. You need a real database to verify the CREATE TABLE DDL actually executed, the table structure is correct, and subsequent migrations can insert into it. Mocking the table creation would hide whether the runner tried to query a non-existent table or malformed the DDL.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a completely fresh PostgreSQL test database with no _migrations table. Execute the runner with at least one migration file. Verify by querying the database: (a) the _migrations table exists with the correct schema (columns for migration name, applied timestamp, etc.), (b) the table is populated with the applied migrations in the correct order, (c) the first migration's record is present and valid, and (d) subsequent queries to _migrations during the run would have succeeded. Verify no 'table does not exist' errors were thrown.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Failure is database schema state. Must inspect actual database to confirm table exists with correct structure. A mock would hide malformed DDL or missing initialization logic.
